name: Henry mining district
short_description: Historic mining district on BLM land in central Utah offering opportunities to find garnets, topaz, and quartz specimens. No permit required for personal collecting.
description:
The Henry mining district sits in the heart of Utah's mineral-rich Sevier County, an area with a rich history of small-scale mining operations dating back over a century. This BLM-managed location offers rockhounders the chance to explore former mining areas and surrounding terrain where garnet, topaz, beryl, and quartz specimens occur naturally in pegmatitic and metamorphic rocks. The district benefits from its location near the transition zone between the Sevier Valley and the High Plateaus, creating unique geological conditions that have concentrated these minerals in accessible locations.

Collecting here is straightforward and beginner-friendly - no permits are required for personal use rockhounding on BLM land. The terrain varies from gentle slopes to moderate hillsides with exposed rock outcrops that make specimen hunting relatively productive. Many collectors focus on the areas around old prospect pits and mineralized outcrops where previous mining activity has exposed fresh material.

The Henry district is particularly known for producing quality garnet specimens ranging from small crystals to larger pieces suitable for display or cutting. Topaz finds are less common but occur in certain pegmatitic zones. The open BLM access means you can explore multiple microenvironments within the district to find your preferred specimen types. Spring and fall offer ideal collecting conditions with comfortable temperatures and lower risk of afternoon thunderstorms.
history: The Henry mining district developed during Utah's early 20th-century prospecting boom when miners sought valuable minerals in the Sevier County uplands. The area's garnets, topaz, and other gemstones formed during Precambrian and Paleozoic metamorphic events when regional heat and pressure transformed parent rocks, followed by later pegmatitic intrusions that crystallized beryl and other minerals in distinct zones.
tips: Focus your search on exposed rocky hillsides and around old prospect pits where erosion has naturally concentrated specimens and previous mining has exposed fresh material. Bring a rock hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from pegmatitic zones rather than just surface picking. Look for reddish-brown garnet crystals in metamorphic rock outcrops and pale yellow or colorless topaz in weathered pegmatite zones. Visit after spring snowmelt in May or early June when runoff has cleared away loose material and exposed new specimens.
terrain_notes: The Henry district sits at elevation around 9,000-10,000 feet with rolling terrain featuring exposed rock outcrops, scattered piñon and juniper vegetation, and several old mining roads and pathways. Terrain is moderately steep in places with uneven rocky footing. Walking distances from parking areas vary from 0.25 to 1.5 miles depending on which zone you access. The high elevation means afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer months.
best_season: May - October, with peak conditions in September - October when afternoon thunderstorm risk decreases and temperatures remain moderate. Summer visits are possible but afternoon monsoon activity increases hazard risk. Early spring can be muddy from snowmelt.
written_directions: From Sevier, take US 89 south toward Marysvale approximately 12 miles. Watch for Forest Service Road markers and old mining district signs on the east side of the highway in the foothills below the High Plateaus. Turn east onto marked dirt roads leading into the mining district. Multiple pullouts and informal parking areas exist near accessible rock outcrops and old prospect sites. GPS coordinates 38.524648, -112.239927 mark the general district center. High-clearance vehicle recommended but not always required depending on specific site accessed.
road_conditions: Dirt and gravel roads in variable condition, some washboarding common. Passenger vehicles can access main areas, though high-clearance or 4x4 preferred for rougher spurs. Check conditions seasonally as roads can become impassable after heavy rain or snowmelt.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and cleared areas exist throughout the district near old mining sites. No developed parking lot; expect rustic conditions. Multiple small parking areas scattered across the district allow access to different collecting zones.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation. Respect old mining infrastructure and do not disturb mine structures or shafts. Pack out all trash and leave the area as you found it.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Respect old mining sites and infrastructure
Pack out all trash
Stay on marked roads and established pathways
nearest_services: Sevier town (about 15 miles north via US 89) has gas, food, water, and services. Marysvale (12 miles south) offers limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain with moderate slopes and uneven rocky footing. Most collecting areas are within short walking distances of parking (under 1 mile). No wheelchair access on dirt roads or rocky outcrops. Sturdy hiking boots recommended for rocky terrain. High elevation (9,000+ feet) may cause exertion for those unaccustomed to altitude.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at the Henry mining district in Utah?
faq.answer: The Henry mining district is known for producing garnet, topaz, beryl, and quartz specimens. Garnets are the most commonly found, with reddish-brown crystals occurring in metamorphic rock outcrops throughout the district. Topaz is less common but can be found in pegmatitic zones, and beryl occasionally appears in similar pegmatite deposits.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at the Henry mining district?
faq.answer: No permit is required to rockhound at the Henry mining district as it is BLM public land open for recreational collecting. You do not need to pay any fees or file paperwork. You simply follow the personal use collection rules: 25 lbs maximum per day, no commercial use, and no motorized excavation.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect specimens at the Henry mining district?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding seasons at the Henry mining district are September - October and May - June. Fall offers cooler temperatures, lower thunderstorm risk, and good visibility of specimens cleared by spring snowmelt. Summer visits are possible but afternoon monsoon activity increases hazard risk. Winter can bring snow at this 9,000+ foot elevation.

faq.question: Is the Henry mining district family-friendly and suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, the Henry mining district is excellent for families and beginner rockhounders. The terrain is relatively gentle with easy hiking access to many collecting sites. Kids as young as 6 can participate. No special skills or equipment are required beyond basic hand tools like a hammer and chisel. Specimens are visible on the surface and easy to spot once you know what to look for.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring rockhounding at the Henry mining district?
faq.answer: Bring a rock hammer, cold chisel, and small pry bar to carefully extract specimens from rock outcrops. Pack plenty of water (you are at 9,000+ feet elevation), a backpack to carry your finds, sturdy hiking boots for rocky terrain, and sunscreen. A hand lens is useful for examining crystals. Bring trash bags to pack out everything you bring in.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog rockhounding at the Henry mining district?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are welcome on this BLM public land. Keep your dog under control and be aware of wildlife hazards including snakes (which can occur at this elevation) and loose abandoned mine shafts scattered throughout the district. Bring extra water for your dog as the high elevation and rocky terrain make for strenuous hiking.
